main_question:
He then crossed the Balkan Mountains, and took Adrianople. General Paskjevitch, in the meantime, was most fortunate in Asia. After the victorious battles of Konouly and Millidust, he had taken Erzerum, and repulsed the Pasha of Trebizond in every direction. [masked sentence describing an inference or effect]

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing an inference or effect]:
answer_strings: The Sultan, in consequence of all these reverses, was obliged to yield, and peace was at last established by the Treaty of Adrianople of the 14th September, 1829.
The Sultan, in consequence of all these successes, was enabled to persist, and war continued despite the Treaty of Adrianople of the 14th September, 1829.

He, therefore, when the rising of the Maghyars threatened to make an end of the Austrian Empire, sent an army of 170,000 men, under the command of Paskjevitch, Sass, and Rüdiger, into Hungary, by which he promptly suppressed the movement, and delivered many of the noblest patriots to the scaffold, prepared for them by a government, which, unaided, could not have resisted their struggle for liberty and independence.
In consequence of this glorious success, the senate entreated Peter to accept the title of Emperor and "the Great," to which request he graciously consented.
Thus, the combined successes of these generals had a significant consequence on the overall campaign and led to Russian control over key strategic areas.
Consequently, the Ottoman Empire's morale collapsed, prompting the Pasha of Trebizond to seek a swift capitulation, while Russian forces pressed forward with confidence toward the heart of empire.
passage: He then crossed the Balkan Mountains, and took Adrianople. General Paskjevitch, in the meantime, was most fortunate in Asia. After the victorious battles of Konouly and Millidust, he had taken Erzerum, and repulsed the Pasha of Trebizond in every direction. The Sultan, in consequence of all these reverses, was obliged to yield, and peace was at last established by the Treaty of Adrianople of the 14th September, 1829.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Land of the Czar, an ethnology published in 1875 by O. W. Wahl, an unknown unknown.
main_question:
It is placed under the direction of a head-herdsman, or taboontchick, who, with his assistants, attends to all the management of the horses and to their safety from horse-thieves and wolves. As he is responsible for every horse of his herd, his vigilance and constant application are such as only a taboontchick (chosen mostly from the Cossacks) could stand. He knows no sheltering roof, but must be on the alert night and day, [masked clause describing a condition or proviso]. Sleeping, so to say, with one eye open, he must be ready on the slightest alarm to spring into the saddle and assist the brave stallions in their defence of mares and foals against HORSES.

answer_strings: if he would not risk the loss of the hard-won earnings of the whole year
if he dared risk the loss of the hard-won earnings of the whole year
unless driven to it by hunger
if only the pole and carriage-wheels are prosti-Russki fashion
unless he is certain there is no danger to his herd
provided that no storm or wolf approaches, his vigilance must never waver, lest danger strikes

As he is responsible for every horse of his herd, his vigilance and constant application are such as only a taboontchick (chosen mostly from the Cossacks) could stand. He knows no sheltering roof, but must be on the alert night and day, if he would not risk the loss of the hard-won earnings of the whole year. Sleeping, so to say, with one eye open, he must be ready on the slightest alarm to spring into the saddle and assist the brave stallions in their defence of mares and foals against HORSES.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Land of the Czar, an ethnology published in 1875 by O. W. Wahl, an unknown unknown.
main_question:
These Tchouds had been probably driven out of their original country by other neighbours, who also have left their own particular traces; for in some places on the banks of the Yenisseï and the Touba have been found many inscriptions altogether different from those of the Tchouds, and which seem to belong to the time of Tchinggis-Khan. Remnants of the wild hordes of Asia still roam about Siberia, and are called Tartars, although they are not all of Turkish origin, [masked clause revising an implied expectation]. We know little about these tribes errant, and the chances of a closer acquaintance are daily diminishing, owing to the growing Russian colonization, which threatens soon to absorb all these tribes within its own numbers.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause revising an implied expectation]:
answer_strings: but are strongly mixed with Finnish and Mongolian elements
but are distinct from Finnish and Mongolian elements
but nobody has as yet succeeded in deciphering the inscriptions
but their houses are palaces compared with the "tchoums" of the Samoyedes
however, they are not always easy to identify
but many are mixed, not purely Turkish indeed
passage: These Tchouds had been probably driven out of their original country by other neighbours, who also have left their own particular traces; for in some places on the banks of the Yenisseï and the Touba have been found many inscriptions altogether different from those of the Tchouds, and which seem to belong to the time of Tchinggis-Khan. Remnants of the wild hordes of Asia still roam about Siberia, and are called Tartars, although they are not all of Turkish origin, but are strongly mixed with Finnish and Mongolian elements. We know little about these tribes errant, and the chances of a closer acquaintance are daily diminishing, owing to the growing Russian colonization, which threatens soon to absorb all these tribes within its own numbers.

In consequence of a second defeat on the 25th September, he retreated to the southern bank of the Araxes. After many indecisive engagements, General Pankratiev succeeded in seducing several of the allies of Persia from their allegiance, [masked clause describing a cause or reason].

passage: In consequence of a second defeat on the 25th September, he retreated to the southern bank of the Araxes. Paskjevitch, successor of Yermalov, carried the war on to the Persian soil, and began by storming the fortified convent of Etchmadsin. After many indecisive engagements, General Pankratiev succeeded in seducing several of the allies of Persia from their allegiance, owing to which, on the 6th July, the Persians lost the battle of Nakhitshevan, on the 7th the fortress of Abbas Adad, and soon after the battle of Abaran.

main_question:
There are now only about 16,500 individuals of this tribe in Russia, who are all Mohammedans, and occupying themselves with the breeding of cattle and horses. In 1860 they were still living, to the number of 40,000, in the north-western district of the Azov country, but these, together with the Crimean Tartars (80,000), have since emigrated to Turkey. [masked sentence revising an implied expectation]

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence revising an implied expectation]:
answer_strings: It is said, however, that many of them have since come back.
It is said, however, that few of them have since come back.

They have, however, cause to repent of their rash piety, for the holy soil did not offer them anything like what they had left behind; and it is said that they are returning in large numbers to the meat-pots of Crimean Egypt.
They have however (since 1865) emigrated in large numbers to Turkey.
Yet, their population has dwindled significantly over time.
Nevertheless, their numbers have shrunk, leaving only a few thousand in Russia.
passage: There are now only about 16,500 individuals of this tribe in Russia, who are all Mohammedans, and occupying themselves with the breeding of cattle and horses. In 1860 they were still living, to the number of 40,000, in the north-western district of the Azov country, but these, together with the Crimean Tartars (80,000), have since emigrated to Turkey. It is said, however, that many of them have since come back.

235 Personal experience in the intercourse with representatives of all classes of Russian society, military and civil, have never afforded the writer a single instance of vindictive feelings existing in Russian minds with regard to England and its successes in the Crimea. On the contrary, they invariably seemed to look upon that war, [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]. Unlike another nation, nearer home, they did not dream of vengeance, but with magnanimous ingenuity seemed to acknowledge the superiority of political and social advancement of their former adversaries, whose example, which had been so forcibly set before them, they ought to follow in more than one respect.

although in good society they resemble those of other countries

although it was fought on their own soil
although the conflict left deep scars, they remained free of bitterness toward England in their hearts
passage: 235 Personal experience in the intercourse with representatives of all classes of Russian society, military and civil, have never afforded the writer a single instance of vindictive feelings existing in Russian minds with regard to England and its successes in the Crimea. On the contrary, they invariably seemed to look upon that war, though disastrous to their arms, as a natural and necessary phase of their political development. Unlike another nation, nearer home, they did not dream of vengeance, but with magnanimous ingenuity seemed to acknowledge the superiority of political and social advancement of their former adversaries, whose example, which had been so forcibly set before them, they ought to follow in more than one respect.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Land of the Czar, an ethnology published in 1875 by O. W. Wahl, an unknown unknown.
main_question:
In 1632 he opened another campaign against Poland, in consequence of which he lost all former conquests, and had to give up all claims on Livonia, Esthonia, and Courland; but, on the other hand, the Poles formally abandoned their claims to the Russian throne. He signed also a treaty with the Grand Sultan, by which the latter promised to prevent the Khan of the Crimea from any renewed invasions of the Russian territories. Michael proved himself to have been a most able politician, and considerably added to the extension of the empire, [masked clause describing an inference or effect]. He died in the year 1646.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing an inference or effect]:
answer_strings: so that at his death his own son saw himself master of a country not less than 255,000 square miles in extent
so that at his death his own son found himself subject to a ruler over an expanse no greater than 255,000 square miles
so that all that can remind one now of Lithuanian nationality is limited to language, certain peculiarities of costume, and some isolated specimens of affinity to their ancient paganism
so Russia then became free from both Polish and Tatar invasions
so the realm grew in both breadth and influence, securing a lasting legacy for future generations, and establishing a precedent for governance
passage: In 1632 he opened another campaign against Poland, in consequence of which he lost all former conquests, and had to give up all claims on Livonia, Esthonia, and Courland; but, on the other hand, the Poles formally abandoned their claims to the Russian throne. He signed also a treaty with the Grand Sultan, by which the latter promised to prevent the Khan of the Crimea from any renewed invasions of the Russian territories. Michael proved himself to have been a most able politician, and considerably added to the extension of the empire, so that at his death his own son saw himself master of a country not less than 255,000 square miles in extent. He died in the year 1646.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Land of the Czar, an ethnology published in 1875 by O. W. Wahl, an unknown unknown.
main_question:
The aged, the decrepit beggar at his door, even the wretched criminal dragging his chains to a Siberian prison, never go empty from his village; for even the poorest villager will fumble for some stray coppers in his pocket, in order that he too may contribute his mite towards the relief of his fellow-man. The Russian cares little for his parental hearth, or for the parish soil he helps to cultivate. [masked sentence describing a cause or reason] In their villages the Russians feel themselves intimately bound only to their families, their neighbours, to the parish, or to individuals, but not to the ground or the place they inhabit; and in this respect they strikingly differ from the Germanic and Latin races, and even from the western Slavonians, in whom the love of the paternal hearth is inextinguishable.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing a cause or reason]:
answer_strings: The cause of this indifference is the absence of landed property amongst the peasants.
The cause of this indifference is the presence of landed property amongst the peasants.

They have, however, cause to repent of their rash piety, for the holy soil did not offer them anything like what they had left behind; and it is said that they are returning in large numbers to the meat-pots of Crimean Egypt.
They curse the She'ites, because they fancy themselves to be Sounnites, but they have not the remotest conception of the difference.
The cause of this is because the Russian's sense of community and shared humanity extends far beyond just their family and home.
Because their ancestors migrated constantly, Russians view land as transient, not ancestral, but family.
passage: The aged, the decrepit beggar at his door, even the wretched criminal dragging his chains to a Siberian prison, never go empty from his village; for even the poorest villager will fumble for some stray coppers in his pocket, in order that he too may contribute his mite towards the relief of his fellow-man. The Russian cares little for his parental hearth, or for the parish soil he helps to cultivate. The cause of this indifference is the absence of landed property amongst the peasants. In their villages the Russians feel themselves intimately bound only to their families, their neighbours, to the parish, or to individuals, but not to the ground or the place they inhabit; and in this respect they strikingly differ from the Germanic and Latin races, and even from the western Slavonians, in whom the love of the paternal hearth is inextinguishable.
